context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on this philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I kind of get that act consequentialism says to look at outcomes, but then all the trolley-type cases start blurring together for me. Could you just help me make sense of the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—let’s unblur it by separating the question into 3 steps.

1. For act consequentialism: what is the basic test for whether an act is right?
2. In each case, what are the outcomes of each option?
3. After that, ask: does that verdict feel right in all the cases, or is there a pattern where it seems mistaken?

Start with step 1 in your own words: how would you state the act consequentialist rule without mentioning any specific trolley case yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than explaining the full solution. Since the student is confused about multiple cases blurring together, I will narrow the task into a comparison framework that moves directly toward the assignment: first identify the act consequentialist test, then apply it case by case, then evaluate whether that view seems right, and finally push toward a more general principle. I will not provide a finished argument or write any part of the assignment for the student.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like: the right act is just whichever one leads to the best overall outcome? But then I immediately get fuzzy about what counts as “best” here, and whether it’s really that simple in cases where I’m directly killing someone.
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. For act consequentialism, don’t jump to “directly” yet—that may matter later, but first ask what the theory counts.

In this assignment’s setup, if the choice is literally 1 death versus 5 deaths, what would the theory initially treat as the better outcome, and why?

Then ask yourself: does that same reasoning apply equally to Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll affirm the student’s partial understanding, then sharpen the concept with a targeted Socratic question. To avoid giving the answer directly, I’ll ask them to operationalize “best” in the specific context of the assignment and compare outcomes before introducing complications like directness. This keeps the discussion on-theme and moves them toward applying act consequentialism to the cases.

exchanges.student: I guess it would say 1 death is better than 5 deaths, just because fewer people die overall. But that’s where I get lost, because then it feels like it should give the same answer in basically every case with that 1-versus-5 structure, and I can’t tell if I’m supposed to just say that or explain why some of them seem different.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the key tension, yes. Act consequentialism tends to flatten the cases if the outcomes are the same.

So the next question is: what feature makes some 1-for-5 cases feel different to you, even when the body count is identical?

Pick two cases that seem morally different to you and name the difference as precisely as you can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core consequentialist verdict and the main philosophical tension. I should now guide them to the assignment’s evaluative component: distinguish the theory’s uniform answer from our intuitions about different cases, without supplying those intuitions for them. A comparison question will help them generate the relevant contrast themselves and move toward a general account.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly where I’m muddled, because I can sort of feel that pushing someone or using someone’s body seems worse than flipping a switch, but I don’t know if that’s just emotion or if there’s a real principle there. And with Fuel/Blood I’m not even sure which feature I’m supposed to notice, so I’m having trouble picking just two cases cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
Then don’t try to solve all the cases at once. Test a few possible differences:

- Is the one person used as a means, or merely harmed as a side effect?
- Is there personal force/contact, or is that a distraction?
- Is someone’s body/resource being taken without consent?

Which of those seems to best separate the cases for you? Pick one and try it on switch vs pushing first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck distinguishing morally relevant features across cases. I should reduce cognitive load by giving a comparison framework rather than content. I will not state the principle or verdicts, but I will direct them to test a few candidate features—means vs side effect, agency/contact, rights/consent—because that helps them identify the relevant contrast themselves. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and on assignment.

exchanges.student: I think “used as a means” maybe fits best, but I’m not confident because I keep mixing it up with just “closer” or more personal violence. In switch it seems like the one person is more like a side effect, and in pushing they’re basically the tool for stopping the trolley, but I’m not sure how to say that without oversimplifying everything else.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a promising distinction. Don’t worry about perfect wording yet—test whether it actually explains more cases.

Ask yourself: in the cases from lecture, where is the one person part of the rescue plan itself, and where is the death just a byproduct of carrying it out?

Try applying that to one of Fuel or Blood. Which way does your distinction classify it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making the central distinction. I should reinforce that progress and push them to test whether their candidate principle can explain more than one pair of cases. I will avoid polishing their language or giving a full formulation, and instead ask them to apply the distinction to another case from the lecture, which advances the assignment toward a general account.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly still not sure because I’m half-remembering those cases, and that’s making the whole distinction feel slippery again. Could you give me a fuller walkthrough of how act consequentialism handles all of Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, and then what the main objection is supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor:
Not a full walkthrough—I’m your tutor, not a substitute for the lecture. But we can make it manageable.

Make a 3-column list for each case: options, who dies under each option, and which option act consequentialism would favor.

Then ask: in any case where the outcome numbers match, what else seems morally important?

Start by giving me your best 1-sentence memory of Fuel and Blood, even if it’s rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fuller walkthrough, but I must not provide a complete solution. I should keep the focus on the assignment and use guided discovery. Since the student is fuzzy on the cases themselves, the most helpful minimal intervention is to ask them to reconstruct each case in a simple comparison table and then guide them to see the consequence-based pattern and the possible objection. This avoids directly supplying the content while moving toward the solution.

exchanges.student: My memory is really shaky, which is why I keep wanting the bigger explanation. I think one of them is about diverting some danger so fewer people die, and the other is about taking something from one person to save several, but I’m not sure I’m even remembering the setup right.
exchanges.tutor:
You don’t need perfect recall to make progress. Bigger picture: your paper needs a structure, not photographic memory.

Try this: if a case involves redirecting a threat, and another involves taking or using one person to save five, would act consequentialism likely treat those differently, or the same, if the totals are still 1 vs 5?

What does your answer suggest the main objection to act consequentialism is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalled by poor recall and is asking for a larger explanation. I should not provide a full solution, but I can reduce the bottleneck by reframing the assignment around structure rather than perfect memory. I’ll guide them to build the argument in layers: the consequentialist core, the contrastive intuition, and the search for a general rule. I will ask a focused question that lets them proceed even with imperfect recall, keeping it bite-sized and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I think it would treat them the same, since it’s still just comparing outcomes, and that’s what keeps tripping me up. So maybe the objection is that it ignores some morally important difference between redirecting harm and intentionally using one person for others, but I’m not sure how to turn that into a clean general account.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the heart of it. Good.

Now push one step further: what would your general account say is usually not allowed—killing as a means, or violating a person’s rights, or something else?

And what would it still allow—foreseen but unintended killing as a side effect?

Give me a rough rule in your own words, even if it’s imperfect.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached the core thesis: act consequentialism treats outcome-equivalent cases alike, and the objection is that it misses a morally relevant distinction. I should affirm this progress, avoid supplying a finished principle, and guide the student to articulate a candidate general account by testing necessary/sufficient conditions. A concise Socratic prompt will move directly toward the assignment’s final part without writing for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe the rule is that it’s not morally okay to kill one person when that person is being used as a means to save the five, or when their rights are being overridden just because it helps the numbers. But it might still be okay if the death is a foreseen side effect of preventing a bigger harm, which I think is the part I’m least sure how to defend.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong draft of the view. The weak point is exactly what you identified: why should side effects be treated differently?

So ask: what is morally different between harming someone as part of your plan and harming someone as a byproduct of stopping a threat?

If you can state that difference clearly, you’ll have the basis for defending your general account. Try it in 2–3 plain sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a plausible candidate principle and now needs help refining and defending it. I should affirm the progress, then test the rule against counterexamples and clarify the weak point without supplying a polished formulation or full defense. A targeted Socratic prompt about what makes side effects different will move them toward justification while staying concise and within the assignment.

exchanges.student: This is where I’m blanking again. I can kind of repeat the distinction, but I don’t really see why it should matter, so I’m worried I’m just memorizing labels instead of understanding the argument.
exchanges.tutor:
Then use this test: in one kind of case, the plan works through that person’s death; in the other, the plan works by stopping the threat, and the death comes along with it.

Why might someone think that first structure shows a deeper disrespect for the individual?

Don’t aim for perfection—just tell me what seems morally worse about making a person part of the mechanism.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and blanking on the justification for the means/side-effect distinction. Per the rules, when the student is expressing frustration, I can be a bit less Socratic and use relevant examples, but still must not give a complete solution or write for them. I will provide a minimal conceptual scaffold by contrasting two action structures—building harm into the plan versus accepting harm while pursuing a separate aim—then ask the student to extract the moral significance in their own words. This keeps the response bite-sized, on-theme, and moves directly toward the assignment’s final argument.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly signals genuine memory gaps about the Fuel and Blood cases (Turns 6-7), the tutor does not diagnose what specifically the student is confused about before responding. Instead, the tutor pivots to a structural framing ('your paper needs a structure, not photographic memory') without checking whether the student's confusion stems from not having done the reading, misremembering details, or conceptual confusion. This is a jump to addressing the issue rather than diagnostic questioning.

By Turn 9, the student had produced a genuinely strong draft of the means/side-effect distinction after struggling earlier. The tutor in Turn 9 affirms it as 'a strong draft' but immediately moves to the next challenge without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or what method led to that improvement. In Turn 10 the student regresses, and the tutor again does not reflect on the earlier success.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor enforces a Socratic approach and declines to give full walkthroughs, but never explicitly explains why this approach supports the student's learning. The tutor says 'I'm your tutor, not a substitute for the lecture' (Turn 6) but does not articulate that the guided-discovery method builds reasoning skills, retention, or long-term philosophical competence.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout: no direct answers are given, key ideas are consistently student-generated, and the tutor resists the student's repeated requests for full walkthroughs. Scaffolding is generally good, with the tutor building on student progress turn by turn. Main deductions are for: (1) failing to diagnose the nature of the student's confusion about Fuel/Blood before pivoting to structural advice; (2) missing a reflection prompt after the student's strong Turn 9 breakthrough; and (3) never explicitly articulating why the guided-discovery approach benefits the student's learning. Dialogue quality and communication quality are strong throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 (Socratic/direct work): No deductions warranted — the tutor never gives submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently withholds conclusions until the student reasons toward them. 1.2 (Scaffolding): A 3-point deduction applies under 1.2.B.a because when the student signals genuine factual memory gaps about the cases in Turns 6-7, the tutor skips diagnostic questioning and jumps to reframing the task structurally, missing an opportunity to identify the root of the confusion. 1.3 (Meta-learning): Two deductions apply — the tutor never explains the rationale for its Socratic constraints (1.3.A.a, -2 pts), and after the student's notable improvement in Turn 9, the tutor does not prompt reflection on what changed in their thinking before moving to the next challenge (1.3.C.a, -2 pts). Dialogue and communication quality are both strong: the tutor stays on-task, uses concise prompts, maintains a supportive tone, and avoids grading language.
